title: Opium

infobox.value: Uncertain, possibly [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"),[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1) or Spain, southern France and northwestern Africa[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* AU: [S8](/wiki/Standard_for_the_Uniform_Scheduling_of_Medicines_and_Poisons#Schedule_8 "Standard for the Uniform Scheduling of Medicines and Poisons") (Controlled drug) * BR: [Class A1](/wiki/Brazilian_Controlled_Drugs_and_Substances_Act#Class_A1 "Brazilian Controlled Drugs and Substances Act") (Narcotic drugs) * CA: [Schedule I](/wiki/Controlled_Drugs_and_Substances_Act#Schedule_I "Controlled Drugs and Substances Act") * DE: [Anlage III](/wiki/Drugs_controlled_by_the_German_Narcotic_Drugs_Act#Anlage_III "Drugs controlled by the German Narcotic Drugs Act") (Special prescription form required) * NZ: [Class B](/wiki/Misuse_of_Drugs_Act_1975#Class_B "Misuse of Drugs Act 1975") * UK: [Class A](/wiki/Drugs_controlled_by_the_UK_Misuse_of_Drugs_Act#Class_A_drugs "Drugs controlled by the UK Misuse of Drugs Act") * US: [Schedule II](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act#Schedule_II_drugs "Controlled Substances Act") * UN: [Narcotic Schedule I](/wiki/Single_Convention_on_Narcotic_Drugs#List_of_controlled_narcotic_drugs "Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs") * Controlled unless and except for medical use under a license.

(also known as **poppy tears**, or **Lachryma papaveris**) is the dried [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") obtained from the seed [capsules](/wiki/Capsule_(fruit) "Capsule (fruit)") of the opium poppy *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*.[[4]](#cite_note-DrugsCom-4) Approximately 12 percent of opium is made up of the [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), which is processed chemically to produce [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") and other synthetic [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") for medicinal use and for the [illegal drug trade](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade "Illegal drug trade"). Opium's main [psychoactive](/wiki/Psychoactive "Psychoactive") [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid"), primarily [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), act on [μ-opioid receptors](/wiki/%CE%9C-opioid_receptor "Μ-opioid receptor"), causing [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia"), with long-term use leading to tolerance and dependence. The latex also contains the closely related [opiates](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate") [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") and [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine"), and non-analgesic [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") such as [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine"). The traditional, labor-intensive method of obtaining the latex is to scratch ("score") the immature seed pods (fruits) by hand; the latex leaks out and dries to a sticky yellowish residue that is later scraped off and dehydrated.

**Opium** (also known as **poppy tears**, or **Lachryma papaveris**) is the dried [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") obtained from the seed [capsules](/wiki/Capsule_(fruit) "Capsule (fruit)") of the opium poppy *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*.[[4]](#cite_note-DrugsCom-4) Approximately 12 percent of opium is made up of the [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), which is processed chemically to produce [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") and other synthetic [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") for medicinal use and for the [illegal drug trade](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade "Illegal drug trade"). Opium's main [psychoactive](/wiki/Psychoactive "Psychoactive") [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid"), primarily [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), act on [μ-opioid receptors](/wiki/%CE%9C-opioid_receptor "Μ-opioid receptor"), causing [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia"), with long-term use leading to tolerance and dependence. The latex also contains the closely related [opiates](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate") [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") and [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine"), and non-analgesic [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") such as [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine"). The traditional, labor-intensive method of obtaining the latex is to scratch ("score") the immature seed pods (fruits) by hand; the latex leaks out and dries to a sticky yellowish residue that is later scraped off and dehydrated.

The English word for opium is [borrowed](/wiki/Loan_word "Loan word") from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), which in turn comes from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): ὄπιον (*ópion*), a [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") of ὀπός (*opós*, 'juice of a plant').[[5]](#cite_note-5) The word *[meconium](/wiki/Meconium "Meconium")* (derived from the Greek for "opium-like", but now used to refer to [newborn](/wiki/Newborn "Newborn") [stools](/wiki/Human_feces "Human feces")) historically referred to related, weaker preparations made from other parts of the opium poppy or different species of poppies.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") region holds the earliest archaeological evidence of human use of opium poppies dating back to before 5000 BCE, with cultivation beginning around 3400 BCE in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). Opium was widely used for food, medicine, ritual, and as a painkiller throughout ancient civilizations including [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), and [Islamic](/wiki/Islamic "Islamic") societies up to [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") times.

The production methods have not significantly changed since ancient times. Through selective breeding of the *Papaver somniferum* plant, the content of the [phenanthrene](/wiki/Phenanthrene "Phenanthrene") alkaloids morphine, codeine, and to a lesser extent thebaine has been greatly increased. In modern times, much of the thebaine, which often serves as the raw material for the synthesis for [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone"), [hydrocodone](/wiki/Hydrocodone "Hydrocodone"), [hydromorphone](/wiki/Hydromorphone "Hydromorphone"), and other [semisynthetic](/wiki/Semisynthesis "Semisynthesis") opiates, originates from extracting *[Papaver orientale](/wiki/Papaver_orientale "Papaver orientale")* or *[Papaver bracteatum](/wiki/Papaver_bracteatum "Papaver bracteatum")*. In preparation for the Second World War, the U.S. Army contracted farmers in the State of [Sinaloa](/wiki/Sinaloa "Sinaloa"), Mexico, to grow opium crops. This was to provide morphine (the gold standard of analgesia) for injured soldiers. Modern opium production, once widely prohibited, still involves large-scale cultivation—especially in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan")—where it is harvested by scoring poppy pods to collect latex used for both illicit drugs and legal medicines, with recent [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban")-led reductions cutting cultivation in Afghanistan by over 95%.

For the illegal drug trade, the [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") is extracted from the opium latex, reducing the bulk weight by 88%. It is then converted to [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") which is almost twice as potent.[[7]](#cite_note-source1986-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The [Mediterranean region](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") contains the earliest archeological evidence of human use; the oldest known seeds date back to more than 5000 BCE in the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") age[[8]](#cite_note-Merlin-8) with purposes such as food, [anaesthetics](/wiki/Anaesthetic "Anaesthetic"), and [ritual](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual"). Evidence from [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") indicates that opium was consumed in several ways, including inhalation of vapors, [suppositories](/wiki/Suppository "Suppository"), [medical poultices](/wiki/Poultice "Poultice"), and as a combination with [hemlock](/wiki/Conium_maculatum "Conium maculatum") for suicide.[[9]](#cite_note-Papadaki-9) Opium is mentioned in the most important [medical](/wiki/History_of_medicine "History of medicine") texts of the ancient and medieval world, including the [Ebers Papyrus](/wiki/Ebers_Papyrus "Ebers Papyrus") and the writings of [Dioscorides](/wiki/Dioscorides "Dioscorides"), [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen"), and [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna"). Widespread medical use of unprocessed opium continued through the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") before giving way to [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and its successors, which could be injected at a precisely controlled dosage.

### Ancient use (pre-500 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient use (pre-500 CE)")]

> A little of it, taken as much as a grain of ervum is a pain-easer, and a sleep-causer, and a digester...but being drank too much it hurts, making men lethargical, and it kills.

[Dioscorides](/wiki/Dioscorides "Dioscorides"), Introduction to *The Herbal of Dioscorides the Greek*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Malwapoppy.jpg/250px-Malwapoppy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malwapoppy.jpg)

Poppy crop from the [Malwa](/wiki/Malwa "Malwa") in India (probably *Papaver somniferum* var. *album*[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1))

Opium has been actively collected since approximately 3400 BC.[[10]](#cite_note-Santella-10)

At least 17 finds of *Papaver somniferum* from [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") settlements have been reported throughout [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), including the placement of large numbers of poppy seed capsules at a burial site (the *Cueva de los Murciélagos*, or "Bat Cave", in Spain), which has been carbon-14 dated to 4200 BCE. Numerous finds of *P. somniferum* or *P. setigerum* from [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") and [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") settlements have also been reported.[[11]](#cite_note-Carr-11) The first known cultivation of opium poppies was in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), approximately 3400 BCE, by [Sumerians](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), who called the plant *hul gil*, the "joy plant".[[12]](#cite_note-Brownstein-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Frontline-13) Tablets found at [Nippur](/wiki/Nippur "Nippur"), a Sumerian spiritual center south of [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), described the collection of poppy juice in the morning and its use in production of opium.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1) Cultivation continued in the Middle East by the [Assyrians](/wiki/Ancient_Assyrians "Ancient Assyrians"), who also collected poppy juice in the morning after scoring the pods with an iron scoop; they called the juice *aratpa-pal*, possibly the root of *Papaver*.[[14]](#cite_note-anon-14) Opium production continued under the [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonians "Babylonians") and [Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt").

Opium was used with [poison hemlock](/wiki/Poison_hemlock "Poison hemlock") to put people quickly and painlessly to death. It was also used in medicine. *[Spongia somnifera](/w/index.php?title=Spongia_somnifera&action=edit&redlink=1 "Spongia somnifera (page does not exist)")*, sponges soaked in opium, were used during surgery.[[12]](#cite_note-Brownstein-12) The Egyptians cultivated *opium thebaicum* in famous poppy fields around 1300 BCE. Opium was traded from Egypt by the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") and [Minoans](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") to destinations around the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), including Greece, [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"), and Europe. By 1100 BCE, opium was cultivated on [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), where surgical-quality knives were used to score the poppy pods, and opium was cultivated, traded, and smoked.[[15]](#cite_note-Kritikos-15) Opium was also mentioned after the [Persian](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") conquest of Assyria and Babylonian lands in the 6th century BC.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Minoan_praying_women_archmus_Heraklion_Crete_Greece.jpg/250px-Minoan_praying_women_archmus_Heraklion_Crete_Greece.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minoan_praying_women_archmus_Heraklion_Crete_Greece.jpg)

*Goddess of Ecstasy* figurine from Crete, dated around the Late Bronze Age

From the earliest finds, opium has appeared to have ritual significance, and anthropologists have speculated ancient priests may have used the drug as a proof of healing power.[[12]](#cite_note-Brownstein-12) In Egypt, the use of opium was generally restricted to priests, magicians, and warriors. Its invention is credited to Thoth, and it was said to have been given by Isis to Ra as treatment for a headache.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1) A figurine of a goddess dated to around 1300 BC from the Minoan period contains three hairpins shaped as poppy capsules, all of which contain slits that suggest the Cretans knew the method of extracting opium. Additionally, her smile and parting lips suggests that she may be in a state induced by the opium. This has influenced some scholars to call her the "goddess of ecstasy".[[16]](#cite_note-Askitopoulou-2002b-16)

Further evidence confirms the use of opium in the Mediterranean since the [Late Bronze Age](/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age "Late Bronze Age") due to a number of small [lekythi](/wiki/Lekythos "Lekythos") from various places in [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"). These lekythi are supposed to have contained pharmaceutical opium due to the shape of the jars being analogous to those of the poppy head. Additionally, each jar is decorated with vertical stripes that are very similar to the process of cutting into the poppy to extract the sap.[[16]](#cite_note-Askitopoulou-2002b-16) There is further evidence for the use of opium during this era due to painted [pyxis](/wiki/Pyxis_(vessel) "Pyxis (vessel)"). These would be decorated with painted poppy capsules and birds holding both poppy capsules and poppy stalks.[[16]](#cite_note-Askitopoulou-2002b-16)

Opium's use in the [ancient Mediterranean](/wiki/History_of_the_Mediterranean_region "History of the Mediterranean region") world is well written about, with many authors discussing its uses. In [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), it was regarded as a magic and poisonous plant that was used in religious ceremonies. Initiates of the [cult of Demeter](/wiki/Demeter "Demeter") would most likely have taken opium.[[17]](#cite_note-Booth-1998-17) This is due to the fact that it is said that Demeter ate the opium plant when her daughter, [Persephone](/wiki/Persephone "Persephone"), was abducted so that she could fall asleep and forget her grief.[[18]](#cite_note-Kririkos-1968-18) For the citizens of [Mycenea](/wiki/Mycenae "Mycenae"), the opium poppy was an object of worship that was depicted on royal tombs.[[18]](#cite_note-Kririkos-1968-18)

Eventually, the Greco-Roman world began to accept opium for its medicinal qualities too. [Helen of Sparta](/wiki/Helen_of_Troy "Helen of Troy") is said to have used the "nepenthes drug" which some authors believe to be a concoction containing opium. Within archaeological sites of ancient [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta"), there have been findings of pendants adorned with opium poppy capsules, affirming this belief.[[18]](#cite_note-Kririkos-1968-18) In the third century BCE, [Theophrastus](/wiki/Theophrastus "Theophrastus") refers to it and discusses the process of obtaining the sap through crushing it, as the process of incising the poppy was lost until 40 CE. The process was relearned by [Scribonius Largus](/wiki/Scribonius_Largus "Scribonius Largus"), physician to the [Emperor Claudius](/wiki/Claudius "Claudius"), who writes about the process of obtaining opium. Both of these authors note that opium induced sleep and numbed pain, disregarding its effects on the brain.[[17]](#cite_note-Booth-1998-17) However, other writings regard its effects on the mind to be important, such as those from [Diagoras of Melos](/wiki/Diagoras_of_Melos "Diagoras of Melos") and [Erasistratus](/wiki/Erasistratus "Erasistratus"), who believed that addiction to opium would harm the brain and body, urging others to not use it at all. [Hippocrates](/wiki/Hippocrates "Hippocrates") believed that opium was a natural remedy that could cure certain ailments, but also advocated for its use sparingly.[[19]](#cite_note-Frontline2-19) Additionally, certain ancient writers also believed poppy to be an important spice. Both [Cato](/wiki/Cato_the_Elder "Cato the Elder") and [Plauto](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus") wrote about its use as a spice, with archaeological evidence from seed cakes being found in ancient Roman farmhouses supporting these claims.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The Greek gods [Hypnos](/wiki/Hypnos "Hypnos") (Sleep), [Nyx](/wiki/Nyx "Nyx") (Night), and [Thanatos](/wiki/Thanatos "Thanatos") (Death) were depicted wreathed in poppies or holding them. Poppies also frequently adorned statues of [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo"), [Asclepius](/wiki/Asclepius "Asclepius"), [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto_(mythology) "Pluto (mythology)"), [Demeter](/wiki/Demeter "Demeter"), [Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite"), [Kybele](/wiki/Kybele "Kybele") and [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), symbolizing nocturnal oblivion.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1)

### Islamic societies (500–1500 CE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Islamic societies (500–1500 CE)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Een_opium_kit_op_Java._TMnr_60009390.jpg/250px-COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Een_opium_kit_op_Java._TMnr_60009390.jpg)](/wiki/File:COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Een_opium_kit_op_Java._TMnr_60009390.jpg)

Opium users in [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") during the [Dutch colonial period](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), c. 1870

As the power of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") declined, the lands to the south and east of the Mediterranean Sea became incorporated into the [Islamic Empires](/wiki/Islamic_Empire "Islamic Empire"). Some Muslims believe *[hadiths](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith")*, such as in *[Sahih Bukhari](/wiki/Sahih_Bukhari "Sahih Bukhari")*, prohibit every intoxicating substance, though the use of intoxicants in medicine has been widely permitted by scholars.[[21]](#cite_note-Syed-21) [Dioscorides'](/wiki/Pedanius_Dioscorides "Pedanius Dioscorides") five-volume *[De Materia Medica](/wiki/De_Materia_Medica "De Materia Medica")*, the precursor of [pharmacopoeias](/wiki/Pharmacopoeia "Pharmacopoeia"), remained in use (which was edited and improved in the Arabic versions[[22]](#cite_note-22)) from the 1st to 16th centuries, and described opium and the wide range of its uses prevalent in the ancient world.[[23]](#cite_note-Berendes-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/%D0%9E%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%83%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%8B.jpg/250px-%D0%9E%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%83%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%8B.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9E%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%83%D0%BC%D0%BE%D0%B5%D0%B4%D1%8B.jpg)

*Opium-eaters* by [Vasily Vereshchagin](/wiki/Vasily_Vereshchagin "Vasily Vereshchagin") depicts an opium den in [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan"), 1868.

Between 400 and 1200 AD, Arab traders introduced opium to China, and to India by 700 AD.[[24]](#cite_note-drugs-24)[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1)[[13]](#cite_note-Frontline-13)[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) The physician [Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Zakariya_al-Razi "Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi") of [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") origin ("Rhazes", 845–930 CE) maintained a laboratory and school in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), and was a student and critic of [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen"); he made use of opium in anesthesia and recommended its use for the treatment of melancholy in *Fi ma-la-yahdara al-tabib*, "In the Absence of a Doctor", a home medical manual directed toward ordinary citizens for self-treatment if a doctor was not available.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The renowned [Andalusian](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") [ophthalmologic](/wiki/Ophthalmology "Ophthalmology") surgeon [Abu al-Qasim al-Zahrawi](/wiki/Abu_al-Qasim_al-Zahrawi "Abu al-Qasim al-Zahrawi") ("Abulcasis", 936–1013 CE) relied on opium and [mandrake](/wiki/Mandragora_officinarum "Mandragora officinarum") as surgical anesthetics and wrote a treatise, *[al-Tasrif](/wiki/Al-Tasrif "Al-Tasrif")*, that influenced medical thought well into the 16th century.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The Persian physician [Abū 'Alī al-Husayn ibn Sina](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna") ("Avicenna") described opium as the most powerful of the stupefacients, in comparison to mandrake and other highly effective herbs, in *[The Canon of Medicine](/wiki/The_Canon_of_Medicine "The Canon of Medicine")*. The text lists medicinal effects of opium, such as analgesia, hypnosis, antitussive effects, gastrointestinal effects, cognitive effects, respiratory depression, neuromuscular disturbances, and sexual dysfunction. It also refers to opium's potential as a poison. Avicenna describes several methods of delivery and recommendations for doses of the drug.[[29]](#cite_note-Heydari-29) This classic text was translated into Latin in 1175 and later into many other languages and remained authoritative until the 19th century.[[30]](#cite_note-Smith-30) [Şerafeddin Sabuncuoğlu](/wiki/%C5%9Eerafeddin_Sabuncuo%C4%9Flu "Şerafeddin Sabuncuoğlu") used opium in the 14th-century Ottoman Empire to treat [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine") headaches, [sciatica](/wiki/Sciatica "Sciatica"), and other painful ailments.[[31]](#cite_note-Ganidagli-31)

### Reintroduction to Western medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reintroduction to Western medicine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Canons_of_medicine.JPG/250px-Canons_of_medicine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Canons_of_medicine.JPG)

Latin translation of [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna")'s *Canon of Medicine*, 1483

Manuscripts of [Pseudo-Apuleius](/wiki/Pseudo-Apuleius "Pseudo-Apuleius")'s 5th-century work from the 10th and 11th centuries refer to the use of wild poppy *[Papaver agreste](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_agreste&action=edit&redlink=1 "Papaver agreste (page does not exist)")* or *[Papaver rhoeas](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas")* (identified as *P. silvaticum*) instead of *P. somniferum* for inducing sleep and relieving pain.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The use of [Paracelsus](/wiki/Paracelsus "Paracelsus")' [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum") was introduced to Western medicine in 1527, when Philippus Aureolus Theophrastus Bombastus von Hohenheim, better known by the name Paracelsus, claimed (dubiously) to have returned from wanderings in Arabia with a famous sword, within the pommel of which he kept "Stones of Immortality" compounded from opium thebaicum, citrus juice, and "quintessence of gold".[[13]](#cite_note-Frontline-13)[[33]](#cite_note-nzepc.auckland.ac.nz-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The name "Paracelsus" was a pseudonym signifying him the equal or better of [Aulus Cornelius Celsus](/wiki/Aulus_Cornelius_Celsus "Aulus Cornelius Celsus"), whose text, which described the use of opium or a similar preparation, had recently been translated and reintroduced to medieval Europe.[[35]](#cite_note-paracelsus-35) *[The Canon of Medicine](/wiki/The_Canon_of_Medicine "The Canon of Medicine")*, the standard medical textbook that Paracelsus burned in a public bonfire three weeks after being appointed professor at the [University of Basel](/wiki/University_of_Basel "University of Basel"), also described the use of opium, though many Latin translations were of poor quality.[[33]](#cite_note-nzepc.auckland.ac.nz-33) *[Laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum")* was originally the 16th-century term for a medicine associated with a particular physician that was widely well-regarded, but became standardized as "[tincture](/wiki/Tincture "Tincture") of opium", a solution of opium in [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), which Paracelsus has been credited with developing.[[24]](#cite_note-drugs-24) During his lifetime, Paracelsus was viewed as an adventurer who challenged the theories and mercenary motives of contemporary medicine with dangerous chemical therapies, but his therapies marked a turning point in Western medicine. In the 1660s, laudanum was recommended for pain, sleeplessness, and diarrhea by [Thomas Sydenham](/wiki/Thomas_Sydenham "Thomas Sydenham"),[[36]](#cite_note-Harding-36) the renowned "father of English medicine" or "English Hippocrates", to whom is attributed the quote, "Among the remedies which it has pleased Almighty God to give to man to relieve his sufferings, none is so universal and so efficacious as opium."[[37]](#cite_note-Enersen-37) Use of opium as a cure-all was reflected in the formulation of [mithridatium](/wiki/Mithridatium "Mithridatium") described in the 1728 *[Chambers Cyclopedia](/wiki/Cyclopaedia,_or_Universal_Dictionary_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Cyclopaedia, or Universal Dictionary of Arts and Sciences")*, which included true opium in the mixture.

Eventually, laudanum became readily available and extensively used by the 18th century in Europe, especially England.[[38]](#cite_note-Lomax-38) Compared to other chemicals available to 18th century regular physicians, opium was a benign alternative to arsenic, mercury, or emetics, and it was remarkably successful in alleviating a wide range of ailments. Due to the constipation often produced by the consumption of opium, it was one of the most effective treatments for cholera, dysentery, and diarrhea. As a cough suppressant, opium was used to treat bronchitis, tuberculosis, and other respiratory illnesses. Opium was additionally prescribed for rheumatism and insomnia.[[39]](#cite_note-Aurin-39) Medical textbooks even recommended its use by people in good health, to "optimize the internal equilibrium of the human body".[[24]](#cite_note-drugs-24)

During the 18th century, opium was found to be a good remedy for nervous disorders. Due to its sedative and tranquilizing properties, it was used to quiet the minds of those with psychosis, help with people who were considered insane, and also to help treat patients with insomnia.[[40]](#cite_note-KramerJohn-40) However, despite its medicinal values in these cases, it was noted that in cases of psychosis, it could cause anger or depression, and due to the drug's euphoric effects, it could cause depressed patients to become more depressed after the effects wore off because they would get used to being high.[[40]](#cite_note-KramerJohn-40)

The standard medical use of opium persisted well into the 19th century. US president [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") was treated with opium in 1841, and in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), the Union Army used 175,000 lb (80,000 kg) of opium tincture and powder and about 500,000 opium pills.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1) During this time of popularity, users called opium "God's Own Medicine".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

One reason for the increase in opiate consumption in the United States during the 19th century was the prescribing and dispensing of legal opiates by physicians and pharmacists to women with "female complaints" (mostly to relieve menstrual pain and [hysteria](/wiki/Female_hysteria "Female hysteria")).[[39]](#cite_note-Aurin-39) Because opiates were viewed as more humane than punishment or restraint, they were often used to treat the mentally ill. Between 150,000 and 200,000 opiate addicts lived in the United States in the late 19th century and between two-thirds and three-quarters of these addicts were women.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Opium addiction in the later 19th century received a hereditary definition. Dr. George Beard in 1869 proposed his theory of [neurasthenia](/wiki/Neurasthenia "Neurasthenia"), a hereditary nervous system deficiency that could predispose an individual to addiction. Neurasthenia was increasingly tied in medical rhetoric to the "nervous exhaustion" suffered by many a white-collar worker in the increasingly hectic and industrialized U.S. life—the most likely potential clients of physicians.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Recreational use in Europe, the Middle East and the US (11th to 19th centuries)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recreational use in Europe, the Middle East and the US (11th to 19th centuries)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/The_Opium_Seller_%28W._M%C3%BCller%29.jpg/250px-The_Opium_Seller_%28W._M%C3%BCller%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Opium_Seller_(W._M%C3%BCller).jpg)

An artist's view of an Ottoman opium seller

Soldiers returning home from the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") in the 11th to 13th century brought opium with them.[[24]](#cite_note-drugs-24) Opium is said to have been used for recreational purposes from the 14th century onwards in Muslim societies. Ottoman and European testimonies confirm that from the 16th to the 19th centuries Anatolian opium was eaten in Constantinople as much as it was exported to Europe.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 1573, for instance, a Venetian visitor to the Ottoman Empire observed many of the Turkish natives of Constantinople regularly drank a "certain black water made with opium" that makes them feel good, but to which they become so addicted, if they try to go without, they will "quickly die".[[44]](#cite_note-44) From drinking it, dervishes claimed the drugs bestowed them with visionary glimpses of future happiness.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Indeed, the Ottoman Empire supplied the West with opium long before China and India.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Extensive textual and pictorial sources also show that poppy cultivation and opium consumption were widespread in [Safavid](/wiki/Safavid "Safavid") Iran[[47]](#cite_note-47) and [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") India.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: England")]

In England, opium fulfilled a "critical" role, as it did other societies, in addressing multifactorial [pain](/wiki/Pain "Pain"), [cough](/wiki/Cough "Cough"), [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery"), [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea"), as argued by [Virginia Berridge](/wiki/Virginia_Berridge "Virginia Berridge").[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49) A medical panacea of the 19th century, "any respectable person" could purchase a range of hashish pastes and (later) morphine with complementary injection kit.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49)

[Thomas De Quincey](/wiki/Thomas_De_Quincey "Thomas De Quincey")'s *[Confessions of an English Opium-Eater](/wiki/Confessions_of_an_English_Opium-Eater "Confessions of an English Opium-Eater")* (1822), one of the first and most famous [literary accounts of opium addiction](/wiki/Opium_and_Romanticism "Opium and Romanticism") written from the point of view of an addict, details the pleasures and dangers of the drug. In the book, it is not Ottoman, nor Chinese, addicts about whom he writes, but English opium users: "I question whether any Turk, of all that ever entered the paradise of opium-eaters, can have had half the pleasure I had."[[50]](#cite_note-50) De Quincey writes about the great English Romantic poet [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") (1772–1834), whose "[Kubla Khan](/wiki/Kubla_Khan "Kubla Khan")" is also widely considered to be a poem of the opium experience. Coleridge began using opium in 1791 after developing [jaundice](/wiki/Jaundice "Jaundice") and [rheumatic fever](/wiki/Rheumatic_fever "Rheumatic fever"), and became a full addict after a severe attack of the disease in 1801, requiring 80–100 drops of [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum") daily.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: China")]

#### Recreational use in China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recreational use in China")]

See also: [History of opium in China](/wiki/History_of_opium_in_China "History of opium in China") and [Opium den](/wiki/Opium_den "Opium den")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E4%BA%BA%E6%9C%8D%E9%A3%9F%E9%B4%89%E7%89%87%E5%9C%96.PNG/250px-%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E4%BA%BA%E6%9C%8D%E9%A3%9F%E9%B4%89%E7%89%87%E5%9C%96.PNG)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E4%BA%BA%E6%9C%8D%E9%A3%9F%E9%B4%89%E7%89%87%E5%9C%96.PNG)

An opium den in 18th-century China

The earliest clear description of the use of opium as a [recreational drug](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug") in China came from Xu Boling, who wrote in 1483 that opium was "mainly used to aid masculinity, strengthen sperm and regain vigor", and that it "enhances the art of alchemists, sex and court ladies". He also described an expedition sent by the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") [Chenghua Emperor](/wiki/Chenghua_Emperor "Chenghua Emperor") in 1483 to procure opium for a price "equal to that of gold" in [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan") and [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"), where it is close to the western lands of [Xiyu](/wiki/Western_Regions "Western Regions"). A century later, [Li Shizhen](/wiki/Li_Shizhen "Li Shizhen") listed standard medical uses of opium in his renowned *[Compendium of Materia Medica](/wiki/Compendium_of_Materia_Medica "Compendium of Materia Medica")* (1578), but also wrote that "lay people use it for the art of sex," in particular the ability to "arrest seminal emission". This association of opium with sex continued in China until the end of the 19th century.

Opium smoking began as a privilege of the elite and remained a great luxury into the early 19th century. However, by 1861, [Wang Tao](/wiki/Wang_Tao_(19th_century) "Wang Tao (19th century)") wrote that opium was used even by rich peasants, and even a small village without a rice store would have a shop where opium was sold.[[52]](#cite_note-Zheng-52)

Recreational use of opium was part of a civilized and mannered ritual, akin to an [East Asian tea ceremony](/wiki/East_Asian_tea_ceremony "East Asian tea ceremony"), prior to the extensive prohibitions that came later.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49) In places of gathering, often tea shops, or a person's home servings of opium were offered as a form of greeting and politeness. Often served with tea (in China) and with specific and fine utensils and beautifully carved wooden pipes. The wealthier the smoker, the finer and more expensive material used in ceremony.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49) The image of seedy underground, destitute smokers were often generated by anti-opium narratives and became a more accurate image of opium use following the effects of large scale opium prohibition in the 1880s.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49)

#### Prohibitions in China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Prohibitions in China")]

Opium prohibition in China began in 1729, yet was followed by nearly two centuries of increasing opium use. [A massive destruction of opium](/wiki/Destruction_of_opium_at_Humen "Destruction of opium at Humen") by an emissary of the Chinese [Daoguang Emperor](/wiki/Daoguang_Emperor "Daoguang Emperor") in an attempt to stop opium smuggling by the British led to the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") (1839–1842), in which Britain defeated China. After 1860, opium use continued to increase with widespread domestic production in China. By 1905, an estimated 25 percent of the male population were regular consumers of the drug. Recreational use of opium elsewhere in the world remained rare into late in the 19th century, as indicated by ambivalent reports of opium usage.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49) In 1906, 41,000 tons were produced, but because 39,000 tons of that year's opium were consumed in China, overall usage in the rest of the world was much lower.[[53]](#cite_note-McCoy_opium-53) These figures from 1906 have been criticized as overestimates.[[54]](#cite_note-Rewriting_history-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Opium_smokers_China.gif/250px-Opium_smokers_China.gif)](/wiki/File:Opium_smokers_China.gif)

A Chinese opium house photographed in 1902

Smoking of opium came on the heels of [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") smoking and may have been encouraged by a brief [ban on the smoking](/wiki/Smoking_ban "Smoking ban") of tobacco by the Ming emperor. The prohibition ended in 1644 with the coming of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), which encouraged smokers to mix in increasing amounts of opium.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1) In 1705, [Wang Shizhen](/w/index.php?title=Wang_Shizhen_(poet)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wang Shizhen (poet) (page does not exist)") wrote, "nowadays, from nobility and gentlemen down to slaves and women, all are addicted to tobacco." Tobacco in that time was frequently mixed with other herbs (this continues with [clove cigarettes](/wiki/Clove_cigarettes "Clove cigarettes") to the modern day), and opium was one component in the mixture. Tobacco mixed with opium was called *[madak](/wiki/Madak "Madak")* (or *madat*) and became popular throughout China and its seafaring trade partners (such as [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")) in the 17th century.[[52]](#cite_note-Zheng-52) In 1712, [Engelbert Kaempfer](/wiki/Engelbert_Kaempfer "Engelbert Kaempfer") described [addiction](/wiki/Substance_use_disorder "Substance use disorder") to *madak*: "No commodity throughout the [Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") is retailed with greater profit by the [Batavians](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") than opium, which [its] users cannot do without, nor can they come by it except it be brought by the ships of the Batavians from [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and [Coromandel](/wiki/Coromandel_Coast "Coromandel Coast")."[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25)

Fueled in part by the 1729 ban on *madak*, which at first effectively exempted pure opium as a potentially medicinal product, the smoking of pure opium became more popular in the 18th century. In 1736, the smoking of pure opium was described by [Huang Shujing](/wiki/Huang_Shujing "Huang Shujing"), involving a pipe made from bamboo rimmed with silver, stuffed with palm slices and hair, fed by a clay bowl in which a globule of molten opium was held over the flame of an oil lamp. This elaborate procedure, requiring the maintenance of pots of opium at just the right temperature for a globule to be scooped up with a needle-like skewer for smoking, formed the basis of a craft of "paste-scooping" by which servant girls could become prostitutes as the opportunity arose.[[52]](#cite_note-Zheng-52)

#### Chinese diaspora in the West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Chinese diaspora in the West")]

The [Chinese Diaspora](/wiki/Chinese_Diaspora "Chinese Diaspora") in the West (1800s to 1949) first began to flourish during the 19th century due to famine and political upheaval, as well as rumors of wealth to be had outside of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). Chinese emigrants to cities such as [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") brought with them the Chinese manner of opium smoking, and the social traditions of the [opium den](/wiki/Opium_den "Opium den").[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum.org-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [Indian Diaspora](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") distributed opium-eaters in the same way, and both social groups survived as "[lascars](/wiki/Lascar "Lascar")" (seamen) and "[coolies](/wiki/Coolie "Coolie")" (manual laborers). French sailors provided another major group of opium smokers, having gotten the habit while in [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"), where the drug was promoted and monopolized by the colonial government as a source of revenue.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Among white Europeans, opium was more frequently consumed as [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum") or in [patent medicines](/wiki/Patent_medicine "Patent medicine"). Britain's All-India Opium Act of 1878 formalized ethnic restrictions on the use of opium, limiting recreational opium sales to registered Indian opium-eaters and Chinese opium-smokers only and prohibiting its sale to workers from Burma.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Likewise, in San Francisco, Chinese immigrants were permitted to smoke opium, so long as they refrained from doing so in the presence of whites.[[55]](#cite_note-sfmuseum.org-55)

Because of the low social status of immigrant workers, contemporary writers and media had little trouble portraying opium dens as seats of vice, [white slavery](/wiki/Sexual_slavery#White_slavery "Sexual slavery"), gambling, knife- and revolver-fights, and a source for drugs causing deadly overdoses, with the potential to addict and corrupt the white population. By 1919, anti-Chinese riots attacked [Limehouse](/wiki/Limehouse "Limehouse"), the [Chinatown of London](/wiki/Chinatown,_London "Chinatown, London"). Chinese men were deported for playing [keno](/wiki/Keno "Keno") and sentenced to hard labor for opium possession. Due to this, both the immigrant population and the social use of opium fell into decline.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)
