[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Destruction_of_opium_in_1839.jpg/250px-Destruction_of_opium_in_1839.jpg)](/wiki/File:Destruction_of_opium_in_1839.jpg)

[Destruction of opium at Humen](/wiki/Destruction_of_opium_at_Humen "Destruction of opium at Humen"), June 1839

A large scale opium prohibition attempt began in 1729, when the [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") [Yongzheng Emperor](/wiki/Yongzheng_Emperor "Yongzheng Emperor"), disturbed by *[madak](/wiki/Madak "Madak")* smoking at court and carrying out the government's role of upholding [Confucian](/wiki/Confucian "Confucian") virtues, officially prohibited the sale of opium, except for a small amount for medicinal purposes. The ban punished sellers and [opium den](/wiki/Opium_den "Opium den") keepers, but not users of the drug.[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) Opium was banned completely in 1799, and this prohibition continued until 1860.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/William_John_Huggins_-_The_opium_ships_at_Lintin%2C_China%2C_1824.jpg/250px-William_John_Huggins_-_The_opium_ships_at_Lintin%2C_China%2C_1824.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_John_Huggins_-_The_opium_ships_at_Lintin,_China,_1824.jpg)

British opium ships

During the Qing dynasty, China opened itself to foreign trade under the [Canton System](/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System") through the port of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (Canton), with traders from the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") visiting the port by the 1690s. Due to the growing British demand for Chinese tea and the Chinese Emperor's lack of interest in British commodities other than silver, British traders resorted to trade in opium as a high-value commodity for which China was not self-sufficient. The English traders had been purchasing small amounts of opium from India for trade since [Ralph Fitch](/wiki/Ralph_Fitch "Ralph Fitch") first visited in the mid-16th century.[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) Trade in opium was standardized, with production of balls of raw opium, 1.1–1.6 kg (2.4–3.5 lb), 30% water content, wrapped in poppy leaves and petals, and shipped in chests of 60–65 kg (132–143 lb) (one [picul](/wiki/Picul "Picul")).[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) Chests of opium were sold in auctions in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") with the understanding that the independent purchasers would then smuggle it into China.

China had a positive balance sheet in trading with the British, which led to a decrease of the British silver stocks. Therefore, the British tried to encourage Chinese opium use to enhance their balance, and they delivered it from Indian provinces under British control. In India, its cultivation, as well as the manufacture and traffic to China, were subject to the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") (BEIC), as a strict monopoly of the British government.[[63]](#cite_note-63) There was an extensive and complicated system of BEIC agencies involved in the supervision and management of opium production and distribution in India. Bengal opium was highly prized, commanding twice the price of the domestic Chinese product, which was regarded as inferior in quality.[[53]](#cite_note-McCoy_opium-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/British_ships_in_Canton.jpg/250px-British_ships_in_Canton.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_ships_in_Canton.jpg)

British assault on [Canton](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") during the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), May 1841

Some competition came from the newly independent United States, which began to compete in Guangzhou, selling Turkish opium in the 1820s. Portuguese traders also brought opium from the independent Malwa states of western India, although by 1820, the British were able to restrict this trade by charging "pass duty" on the opium when it was forced to pass through Bombay to reach an *[entrepot](/wiki/Entrepot "Entrepot")*.[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25)
Despite drastic penalties and continued prohibition of opium until 1860, opium smuggling rose steadily from 200 chests per year under the [Yongzheng Emperor](/wiki/Yongzheng_Emperor "Yongzheng Emperor") to 1,000 under the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor"), 4,000 under the [Jiaqing Emperor](/wiki/Jiaqing_Emperor "Jiaqing Emperor"), and 30,000 under the [Daoguang Emperor](/wiki/Daoguang_Emperor "Daoguang Emperor").[[64]](#cite_note-64) This illegal sale of opium, which has been called "the most long continued and systematic international crime of modern times",[[65]](#cite_note-65) became one of the world's most valuable single commodity trades, and between 1814 and 1850, sucked out 11 percent of China's money supply.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In response to the ever-growing number of Chinese people becoming addicted to opium, the Qing [Daoguang Emperor](/wiki/Daoguang_Emperor "Daoguang Emperor") took strong action to halt the smuggling of opium, including the seizure of cargo. In 1838, the Chinese Commissioner [Lin Zexu](/wiki/Lin_Zexu "Lin Zexu") [destroyed](/wiki/Destruction_of_opium_at_Humen "Destruction of opium at Humen") 20,000 chests of opium (approximately 2,660,000 pounds) in Guangzhou in a river.[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) Given that a chest of opium was worth nearly [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1,000 in 1800,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] this was a substantial economic loss. The British queen [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"), not willing to replace the cheap opium with costly silver, began the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") in 1840, the British winning Hong Kong and trade concessions in the first of a series of [Unequal Treaties](/wiki/Unequal_Treaties "Unequal Treaties").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The trade and Hong Kong concessions were a result of the 1841 [Convention of Chuenpi](/wiki/Convention_of_Chuenpi "Convention of Chuenpi") (signed by [Qishan](/wiki/Qishan_(official) "Qishan (official)") and [Charles Elliot](/wiki/Charles_Elliot "Charles Elliot")[[67]](#cite_note-p._1424-67) and the 1842 [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") (signed by [Henry Pottinger](/wiki/Henry_Pottinger "Henry Pottinger"), [Keying](/wiki/Keying_(official) "Keying (official)"), and [Yilibu](/wiki/Yilibu "Yilibu")). [[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The opium trade incurred intense enmity from the later British Prime Minister [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone").[[70]](#cite_note-Lodwick2015-70) As a member of Parliament, Gladstone called it "most infamous and atrocious" referring to the opium trade between China and British India in particular.[[71]](#cite_note-Chouvy2009-71) Gladstone was fiercely against both of the [Opium Wars](/wiki/Opium_Wars "Opium Wars") Britain waged in China in the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") initiated in 1840 and the [Second Opium War](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War") initiated in 1857, denounced British violence against Chinese, and was ardently opposed to the British trade in opium to China.[[72]](#cite_note-QuinaultWindscheffel2013-72) Gladstone lambasted it as "[Palmerston's](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston") Opium War" and said that he felt "in dread of the judgments of God upon England for our national iniquity towards China" in May 1840.[[73]](#cite_note-Foxcroft2013-73) A famous speech was made by Gladstone in Parliament against the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War").[[74]](#cite_note-HanesSanello2004-74)[[75]](#cite_note-IIISanello2004-75) Gladstone criticized it as "a war more unjust in its origin, a war more calculated in its progress to cover this country with permanent disgrace".[[76]](#cite_note-Fay2000-76) His hostility to opium stemmed from the effects of opium brought upon his sister Helen.[[77]](#cite_note-Isba2006-77) Due to the First Opium war brought on by [Palmerston](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston"), there was initial reluctance to join the government of Peel on part of Gladstone before 1841.[[78]](#cite_note-Bebbington1993-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/A_busy_stacking_room_in_the_opium_factory_at_Patna%2C_India._L_Wellcome_V0019154.jpg/250px-A_busy_stacking_room_in_the_opium_factory_at_Patna%2C_India._L_Wellcome_V0019154.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_busy_stacking_room_in_the_opium_factory_at_Patna,_India._L_Wellcome_V0019154.jpg)

Storage of opium at a [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") warehouse, c. 1850

Following China's defeat in the [Second Opium War](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War") in 1858, China was forced to legalize opium and began massive domestic production. Importation of opium peaked in 1879 at 6,700 tons, and by 1906, China was producing 85 percent of the world's opium, some 35,000 tons, and 27 percent of its adult male population regularly used opium‍—‌13.5 million people consuming 39,000 tons of opium yearly.[[53]](#cite_note-McCoy_opium-53) From 1880 to the beginning of the Communist era, the British attempted to discourage the use of opium in China, but this effectively promoted the use of morphine, heroin, and cocaine, further exacerbating the problem of addiction.[[49]](#cite_note-Dikotter-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Cover_page_of_The_Truth_About_Opium_Smoking_%281882%29.jpg/250px-Cover_page_of_The_Truth_About_Opium_Smoking_%281882%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cover_page_of_The_Truth_About_Opium_Smoking_(1882).jpg)

The cover page of the book *The Truth about Opium Smoking*

Scientific evidence of the pernicious nature of opium use was largely undocumented in the 1890s, when Protestant [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries") in China decided to strengthen their opposition to the trade by compiling data which would demonstrate the harm the drug did. Faced with the problem that many Chinese associated Christianity with opium, partly due to the arrival of early Protestant missionaries on opium clippers, at the 1890 Shanghai Missionary Conference, they agreed to establish the Permanent Committee for the Promotion of Anti-Opium Societies in an attempt to overcome this problem and to arouse public opinion against the opium trade. The members of the committee were [John Glasgow Kerr](/wiki/John_Glasgow_Kerr "John Glasgow Kerr"), MD, American Presbyterian Mission in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (Canton); [B.C. Atterbury, MD](/wiki/Boudinot_Currie_Atterbury "Boudinot Currie Atterbury"), American Presbyterian Mission in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") (Peking); Archdeacon [Arthur E. Moule](/wiki/Arthur_Evans_Moule "Arthur Evans Moule"), Church Missionary Society in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); Henry Whitney, MD, American Board of Commissioners for foreign Missions in [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou"); the Rev. Samuel Clarke, China Inland Mission in [Guiyang](/wiki/Guiyang "Guiyang"); the Rev. [Arthur Gostick Shorrock](/wiki/Arthur_Gostick_Shorrock "Arthur Gostick Shorrock"), English Baptist Mission in [Taiyuan](/wiki/Taiyuan "Taiyuan"); and the Rev. [Griffith John](/wiki/Griffith_John "Griffith John"), London Mission Society in [Hankou](/wiki/Hankou "Hankou").[[79]](#cite_note-79) These missionaries were generally outraged over the British government's [Royal Commission on Opium](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_Opium "Royal Commission on Opium") visiting India but not China. Accordingly, the missionaries first organized the [Anti-Opium League in China](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Opium_League_in_China&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anti-Opium League in China (page does not exist)") among their colleagues in every mission station in China. American missionary [Hampden Coit DuBose](/wiki/Hampden_Coit_DuBose "Hampden Coit DuBose") acted as first president. This organization, which had elected national officers and held an annual national meeting, was instrumental in gathering data from every Western-trained medical doctor in China, which was then published as [William Hector Park](/w/index.php?title=William_Hector_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "William Hector Park (page does not exist)") compiled *Opinions of Over 100 Physicians on the Use of Opium in China* (Shanghai: American Presbyterian Mission Press, 1899). The vast majority of these medical doctors were missionaries; the survey also included doctors who were in private practices, particularly in Shanghai and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), as well as Chinese who had been trained in medical schools in Western countries. In England, the home director of the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission"), [Benjamin Broomhall](/wiki/Benjamin_Broomhall "Benjamin Broomhall"), was an active opponent of the opium trade, writing two books to promote the banning of opium smoking: *The Truth about Opium Smoking* and *The Chinese Opium Smoker*. In 1888, Broomhall formed and became secretary of the Christian Union for the Severance of the British Empire with the Opium Traffic and editor of its periodical, *National Righteousness*. He lobbied the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") to stop the opium trade. He and [James Laidlaw Maxwell](/wiki/James_Laidlaw_Maxwell "James Laidlaw Maxwell") appealed to the London Missionary Conference of 1888 and the Edinburgh Missionary Conference of 1910 to condemn the continuation of the trade. When Broomhall was dying, his son Marshall read to him from *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* the welcome news that an agreement had been signed ensuring the end of the opium trade within two years.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/China_%26_Opium_1908_en.jpg/250px-China_%26_Opium_1908_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_%26_Opium_1908_en.jpg)

Map showing the amount of opium produced in China in 1908. The quote "We English, by the policy we have pursued, are morally responsible for every acre of land in China which is withdrawn from the cultivation of grain and devoted to that of the poppy; so that the fact of the growth of the drug in China ought only to increase our sense of responsibility." is by [Lord Justice Fry](/wiki/Lord_Justice_Fry "Lord Justice Fry").

Official Chinese resistance to opium was renewed on September 20, 1906, with an antiopium initiative intended to eliminate the drug problem within 10 years. The program relied on the turning of public sentiment against opium, with mass meetings at which [opium paraphernalia](/wiki/Drug_paraphernalia "Drug paraphernalia") were publicly burned, as well as coercive legal action and the granting of police powers to organizations such as the Fujian Anti-Opium Society. Smokers were required to register for licenses for gradually reducing rations of the drug. Action against opium farmers centered upon a highly repressive incarnation of law enforcement in which rural populations had their property destroyed, their land confiscated and/or were publicly tortured, humiliated and executed.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Addicts sometimes turned to missionaries for treatment for their addiction, though many associated these foreigners with the drug trade. The program was counted as a substantial success, with a cessation of direct British opium exports to China (but not Hong Kong)[[81]](#cite_note-81) and most provinces declared free of opium production. Nonetheless, the success of the program was only temporary, with opium use rapidly increasing during the disorder following the death of [Yuan Shikai](/wiki/Yuan_Shikai "Yuan Shikai") in 1916.[[82]](#cite_note-Madancy-82) Opium farming also increased, peaking in 1930 when the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") singled China out as the primary source of illicit opium in East and Southeast Asia. Many[[83]](#cite_note-83) local powerholders facilitated the trade during this period to finance conflicts over territory and political campaigns. In some areas food crops were eradicated to make way for opium, contributing to famines in Guizhou and Shaanxi Provinces between 1921 and 1923, and food deficits in other provinces.

Beginning in 1915, Chinese nationalist groups came to describe the period of military losses and [Unequal Treaties](/wiki/Unequal_Treaties "Unequal Treaties") as the "[Century of National Humiliation](/wiki/Century_of_National_Humiliation "Century of National Humiliation")", later defined to end with the conclusion of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") in 1949.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

In the northern provinces of [Ningxia](/wiki/Ningxia "Ningxia") and [Suiyuan](/wiki/Suiyuan "Suiyuan") in China, [Chinese Muslim](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people") General [Ma Fuxiang](/wiki/Ma_Fuxiang "Ma Fuxiang") both prohibited and engaged in the opium trade. It was hoped that Ma Fuxiang would have improved the situation, since Chinese Muslims were well known for opposition to smoking opium.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Ma Fuxiang officially prohibited opium and made it illegal in Ningxia, but the [Guominjun](/wiki/Guominjun "Guominjun") reversed his policy; by 1933, people from every level of society were abusing the drug, and Ningxia was left in destitution.[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 1923, an officer of the [Bank of China](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China") from [Baotou](/wiki/Baotou "Baotou") found out that Ma Fuxiang was assisting the drug trade in opium which helped finance his military expenses. He earned US$2 million from taxing those sales in 1923. General Ma had been using the bank, a branch of the Government of China's exchequer, to arrange for silver currency to be transported to Baotou to use it to sponsor the trade.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

The opium trade under the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") was important to its finances in the 1940s.[[88]](#cite_note-Matten2011-88) [Peter Vladimirov](/wiki/Peter_Vladimirov "Peter Vladimirov")'s diary provided a first hand account.[[89]](#cite_note-Vladimirov1975-89) [Chen Yung-fa](/wiki/Chen_Yung-fa "Chen Yung-fa") provided a detailed historical account of how the opium trade was essential to the economy of Yan'an during this period.[[90]](#cite_note-SaichVen1995-90) [Mitsubishi](/wiki/Mitsubishi "Mitsubishi") and [Mitsui](/wiki/Mitsui "Mitsui") were involved in the opium trade during the Japanese occupation of China.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

[Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") government is generally credited with eradicating both consumption and production of opium during the 1950s using unrestrained repression and social reform.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) Ten million addicts were forced into compulsory treatment, dealers were executed, and opium-producing regions were planted with new crops. Remaining opium production shifted south of the Chinese border into the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_(Southeast_Asia) "Golden Triangle (Southeast Asia)") region.[[53]](#cite_note-McCoy_opium-53) The remnant opium trade primarily served Southeast Asia, but spread to American soldiers during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"); based on a study of opiate use in soldiers returning to the United States in 1971, 20 percent of participants were dependent enough to experience withdrawal symptoms.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Prohibition outside China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Prohibition outside China")]

There were no legal restrictions on the importation or use of opium in the United States until the San Francisco Opium Den Ordinance, which banned dens for public smoking of opium in 1875, a measure fueled by [anti-Chinese sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Chinese_sentiment "Anti-Chinese sentiment") and the perception that whites were starting to frequent the dens. This was followed by an 1891 California law requiring that narcotics carry warning labels and that their sales be recorded in a registry; amendments to the California Pharmacy and Poison Act in 1907 made it a crime to sell opiates without a prescription, and bans on possession of opium or [opium pipes](/wiki/Opium_pipe "Opium pipe") in 1909 were enacted.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

At the US federal level, the legal actions taken reflected constitutional restrictions under the [enumerated powers](/wiki/Enumerated_powers "Enumerated powers") doctrine prior to reinterpretation of the [commerce clause](/wiki/Commerce_clause "Commerce clause"), which did not allow the federal government to enact arbitrary prohibitions, but did permit arbitrary taxation.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Beginning in 1883, opium importation was taxed at US$6 to US$300 per pound, until the Opium Exclusion Act of 1909 prohibited the importation of opium altogether. In a similar manner, the [Harrison Narcotics Tax Act](/wiki/Harrison_Narcotics_Tax_Act "Harrison Narcotics Tax Act") of 1914, passed in fulfillment of the [First International Opium Convention](/wiki/First_International_Opium_Convention "First International Opium Convention") of 1912, nominally placed a tax on the distribution of opiates, but served as a *de facto* prohibition of the drugs. Today, opium is regulated by the [Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration") under the [Controlled Substances Act](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act "Controlled Substances Act").

The colonies of Queensland and South Australia along with the Northern Territory, where three of the four states of colonial Australia which still had large, yet unknown numbers of Aboriginal people living on Country in regional, remote and very remote locations. From time to time, this made in necessary for laws to be created so Australian’s living within the colony understood their responsibilities to society, and the law was working to ensure protection from potential exploitation, perceived or otherwise. By 1897 the passage of law in all three of the above locations had restricted the sale, exchange or trade of opium with any Aboriginal person without exception, this broad application was required to ensure the law was enforceable.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Hardening of Canadian attitudes toward Chinese opium users and fear of a spread of the drug into the white population led to the effective criminalization of opium for nonmedical use in Canada between 1908 and the mid-1920s.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 1909, the [International Opium Commission](/wiki/International_Opium_Commission "International Opium Commission") was founded, and by 1914, 34 nations had agreed that the production and importation of opium should be diminished. In 1924, 62 nations participated in a meeting of the commission. Subsequently, this role passed to the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), and all signatory nations agreed to prohibit the import, sale, distribution, export, and use of all narcotic drugs, except for medical and scientific purposes. This role was later taken up by the [International Narcotics Control Board](/wiki/International_Narcotics_Control_Board "International Narcotics Control Board") of the United Nations under [Article 23](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Single_Convention_on_Narcotic_Drugs_Article_23:_NATIONAL_OPIUM_AGENCIES "s:Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs Article 23: NATIONAL OPIUM AGENCIES") of the [Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs](/wiki/Single_Convention_on_Narcotic_Drugs "Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs"), and subsequently under the [Convention on Psychotropic Substances](/wiki/Convention_on_Psychotropic_Substances "Convention on Psychotropic Substances"). Opium-producing nations are required to designate a [government agency](/wiki/Government_agency "Government agency") to take physical possession of licit opium crops as soon as possible after harvest and conduct all wholesaling and exporting through that agency.[[1]](#cite_note-Schiff-1)

### Indochina tax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Indochina tax")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/10_peintures_annamites_repr%C3%A9sentant_les_m%C3%A9tiers_au_Tonkin_%28Fumeurs_d%27opium_Annam_-_Tonkin%29.jpg/250px-10_peintures_annamites_repr%C3%A9sentant_les_m%C3%A9tiers_au_Tonkin_%28Fumeurs_d%27opium_Annam_-_Tonkin%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_peintures_annamites_repr%C3%A9sentant_les_m%C3%A9tiers_au_Tonkin_(Fumeurs_d%27opium_Annam_-_Tonkin).jpg)

Two men smoking opium in [Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin_(French_protectorate) "Tonkin (French protectorate)") in 1923

From 1897 to 1902, [Paul Doumer](/wiki/Paul_Doumer "Paul Doumer") (later [President of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France")) was [Governor-General of French Indochina](/wiki/Governor-General_of_French_Indochina "Governor-General of French Indochina"). Upon his arrival the colonies were losing millions of francs each year. Determined to put them on a paying basis he levied taxes on various products, opium among them. The Vietnamese, Cambodians and Laotians who could or would not pay these taxes, lost their houses and land, and often became day laborers. Evidently, resorting to this means of gaining income gave France a vested interest in the continuation of opium use among the population of Indochina.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Regulation in Britain and the United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Regulation in Britain and the United States")]

Before the 1920s, regulation in Britain was controlled by pharmacists. Pharmacists who were found to have prescribed opium for illegitimate uses and anyone found to have sold opium without proper qualifications would be prosecuted.[[100]](#cite_note-100) With the passing of the Rolleston Act in Britain in 1926, doctors were allowed to prescribe opiates such as morphine and heroin if they believed their patients demonstrated a medical need. Because addiction was viewed as a medical problem rather than an indulgence, doctors were permitted to allow patients to wean themselves off opiates rather than cutting off any opiate use altogether.[[101]](#cite_note-BrownRichard-101) The passing of the Rolleston Act put the control of opium use in the hands of medical doctors instead of pharmacists. Later in the 20th century, addiction to opiates, especially heroin in young people, continued to rise and so the sale and prescription of opiates was limited to doctors in treatment centres. If these doctors were found to be prescribing opiates without just cause, then they could lose their licence to practice or prescribe drugs.[[101]](#cite_note-BrownRichard-101)

Abuse of opium in the United States began in the late 19th century and was largely associated with Chinese immigrants. During this time the use of opium had little stigma; the drug was used freely until 1882 when a law was passed to confine opium smoking to specific dens.[[101]](#cite_note-BrownRichard-101) Until the full ban on opium-based products came into effect just after the beginning of the twentieth century, physicians in the US considered opium a miracle drug that could help with many ailments. Therefore, the ban on said products was more a result of negative connotations towards its use and distribution by Chinese immigrants who were heavily persecuted during this particular period in history.[[101]](#cite_note-BrownRichard-101) As the 19th century progressed however, doctor [Hamilton Wright](/wiki/Hamilton_Wright "Hamilton Wright") worked to decrease the use of opium in the US by submitting the Harrison Act to congress. This act put taxes and restrictions on the sale and prescription of opium, as well as trying to stigmatize the opium poppy and its derivatives as "demon drugs", to try to scare people away from them.[[101]](#cite_note-BrownRichard-101) This act and the stigma of a demon drug on opium, led to the criminalization of people that used opium-based products. It made the use and possession of opium and any of its derivatives illegal. The restrictions were recently redefined by the Federal Controlled Substances Act of 1970.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### 20th-century use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 20th-century use")]

Opium production in China and the rest of East Asia was nearly wiped out after [WWII](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"); however, sustained covert support by the United States [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") for the [Thai](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") Northern Army and the Chinese Nationalist [Kuomintang army invading Burma](/wiki/Kuomintang_in_Burma "Kuomintang in Burma") facilitated production and trafficking of the drug from Southeast Asia for decades, with the region becoming a major source of world supplies.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

During the Communist era in Eastern Europe, poppy stalks sold in bundles by farmers were processed by users with household chemicals to make *kompot* ("[Polish heroin](/wiki/Polish_heroin "Polish heroin")"), and poppy seeds were used to produce *koknar*, an opiate.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### Obsolescence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Obsolescence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Apothecary_vessel_Opium_18-19_century.jpg/250px-Apothecary_vessel_Opium_18-19_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apothecary_vessel_Opium_18-19_century.jpg)

Apothecary vessel for storage of opium as a pharmaceutical, Germany, 18th or 19th century

Globally, opium has gradually been superseded by a variety of purified, semi-synthetic, and synthetic [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") with progressively stronger effects, and by other [general anesthetics](/wiki/General_anesthetics "General anesthetics"). This process began in 1804, when [Friedrich Wilhelm Adam Sertürner](/wiki/Friedrich_Sert%C3%BCrner "Friedrich Sertürner") first isolated morphine from the opium poppy.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Morphine10mgmLvial.jpg/250px-Morphine10mgmLvial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morphine10mgmLvial.jpg)

Modern vial of morphine first isolated from opium by Friedrich Sertürner

The process continued until 1817, when Sertürner published his results after thirteen years of research and a nearly disastrous trial on himself and three boys.[[108]](#cite_note-108) The great advantage of purified morphine was that a patient could be treated with a known dose—whereas with raw plant material, as [Gabriel Fallopius](/wiki/Gabriel_Fallopius "Gabriel Fallopius") once lamented, "if soporifics are weak they do not help; if they are strong they are exceedingly dangerous."

Morphine was the first pharmaceutical isolated from a natural product, and this success encouraged the isolation of other alkaloids: by 1820, isolations of [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine"), [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine"), [veratrine](/wiki/Veratrine "Veratrine"), [colchicine](/wiki/Colchicine "Colchicine"), [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), and [quinine](/wiki/Quinine "Quinine") were reported. Morphine sales began in 1827, by [Heinrich Emanuel Merck](/wiki/Heinrich_Emanuel_Merck "Heinrich Emanuel Merck") of Darmstadt, and helped him expand his family pharmacy into the [Merck KGaA](/wiki/Merck_KGaA "Merck KGaA") pharmaceutical company. [Codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") was isolated in 1832 by [Pierre Jean Robiquet](/wiki/Pierre_Jean_Robiquet "Pierre Jean Robiquet").[[109]](#cite_note-109)

The use of [diethyl ether](/wiki/Diethyl_ether "Diethyl ether") and [chloroform](/wiki/Chloroform "Chloroform") for [general anesthesia](/wiki/General_anesthesia "General anesthesia") began in 1846–1847, and rapidly displaced the use of opiates and [tropane](/wiki/Tropane "Tropane") alkaloids from [Solanaceae](/wiki/Solanaceae "Solanaceae") due to their relative safety.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

[Heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), the first semi-synthetic opioid, was first synthesized in 1874, but was not pursued until its rediscovery in 1897 by [Felix Hoffmann](/wiki/Felix_Hoffmann "Felix Hoffmann") at the [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer") pharmaceutical company in [Elberfeld](/wiki/Elberfeld "Elberfeld"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). From 1898 to 1910 heroin was marketed as a non-addictive morphine substitute and cough medicine for children. Because the lethal dose of heroin was viewed as a hundred times greater than its effective dose, heroin was advertised as a safer alternative to other opioids.[[111]](#cite_note-111) By 1902, sales made up 5 percent of the company's profits, and "heroinism" had attracted media attention.[[112]](#cite_note-112) [Oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone"), a [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") derivative similar to [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), was introduced by Bayer in 1916 and promoted as a less-addictive analgesic. Preparations of the drug such as [oxycodone with paracetamol](/wiki/Oxycodone/paracetamol "Oxycodone/paracetamol") and [extended release](/wiki/Extended_release "Extended release") oxycodone remain popular to this day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A range of synthetic [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") such as [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone") (1937), [pethidine](/wiki/Pethidine "Pethidine") (1939), [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl") (late 1950s), and derivatives thereof have been introduced, and each is preferred for certain specialized applications. Nonetheless, morphine remains the drug of choice for American [combat medics](/wiki/Combat_medic "Combat medic"), who carry packs of [syrettes](/wiki/Syrette "Syrette") containing 16 milligrams each for use on severely wounded soldiers.[[113]](#cite_note-MOLLE-113) No drug has been found that can match the painkilling effect of [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") without also duplicating much of their addictive potential.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Modern production and use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Modern production and use")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(October 2022)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Opium_%28Drug%29.jpg/250px-Opium_%28Drug%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opium_(Drug).jpg)

Dried latex from opium poppy

Opium was [prohibited](/wiki/Drug_prohibition "Drug prohibition") in many countries during the early 20th century, leading to the modern pattern of opium production as a precursor for illegal [recreational drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug") or tightly regulated, highly taxed, legal prescription drugs. In 1980, 2,000 tons of opium supplied all legal and illegal uses.[[25]](#cite_note-Trocki-25) Worldwide production in 2006 was 6610 tonnes[[114]](#cite_note-114)—about one-fifth the level of production in 1906; since then, opium production has fallen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2002, the price for one kilogram of opium was US$300 for the farmer, US$800 for purchasers in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), and US$16,000 on the streets of Europe before conversion into heroin.[[115]](#cite_note-2002abc-115)

Opium production increased considerably, surpassing 5,000 tons in 2002 and reaching 8,600 tons in Afghanistan and 840 tons in the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_(Southeast_Asia) "Golden Triangle (Southeast Asia)") in 2014.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") has estimated that current production of opium would need to increase fivefold to account for total global medical need.[[54]](#cite_note-Rewriting_history-54) Solar energy panels in use in Afghanistan have allowed farmers to dig their wells deeper, leading to a bumper crop of opium year after year.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In a 2023 report, poppy cultivation in southern Afghanistan was reduced by over 80% as a result of Taliban campaigns to stop its use toward opium. This included a 99% reduction of opium growth in the [Helmand Province](/wiki/Helmand_Province "Helmand Province").[[119]](#cite_note-119) In November 2023, a U.N report showed that in the entirety of Afghanistan, poppy cultivation dropped by over 95%, removing it from its place as being the world's largest opium producer.[[120]](#cite_note-aljazeera.com-120)[[121]](#cite_note-news.un.org-121)

### *Papaver somniferum*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Papaver somniferum")]

Main article: [Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")

Opium poppies are popular and attractive garden plants, whose flowers vary greatly in color, size and form. A modest amount of domestic cultivation in private gardens is not usually subject to legal controls.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In part, this tolerance reflects variation in addictive potency. A cultivar for opium production, *Papaver somniferum L. elite*, contains 91.2 percent morphine, codeine, and thebaine in its latex alkaloids, whereas in the latex of the condiment cultivar "Marianne", these three alkaloids total only 14.0 percent. The remaining alkaloids in the latter cultivar are primarily [narcotoline](/wiki/Narcotoline "Narcotoline") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine").[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Seed capsules can be dried and used for decorations, but they also contain morphine, codeine, and other alkaloids. These pods can be boiled in water to produce a bitter [tea](/wiki/Poppy_tea "Poppy tea") that induces a long-lasting intoxication. If allowed to mature, poppy pods ([poppy straw](/wiki/Poppy_straw "Poppy straw")) can be crushed and used to produce lower quantities of [morphinans](/wiki/Morphinans "Morphinans"). In poppies subjected to mutagenesis and selection on a mass scale, researchers have been able to use poppy straw to obtain large quantities of [oripavine](/wiki/Oripavine "Oripavine"), a precursor to [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") and antagonists such as [naltrexone](/wiki/Naltrexone "Naltrexone").[[123]](#cite_note-123) Although millennia older, the production of poppy head decoctions can be seen as a quick-and-dirty variant of the Kábáy poppy straw process, which since its publication in 1930 has become the major method of obtaining licit opium alkaloids worldwide, as discussed in [Morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine").

[Poppy seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed") are a common and flavorsome topping for breads and cakes. One gram of poppy seeds contains up to 33 micrograms of morphine and 14 micrograms of codeine, and the [Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration](/wiki/Substance_Abuse_and_Mental_Health_Services_Administration "Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") formerly mandated that all drug screening laboratories use a standard cutoff of 300 nanograms per milliliter in urine samples. A single poppy seed roll (0.76 grams of seeds) usually did not produce a positive [drug test](/wiki/Drug_test "Drug test"), but a positive result was observed from eating two rolls. A slice of poppy seed cake containing nearly five grams of seeds per slice produced positive results for 24 hours. Such results are viewed as [false positive](/wiki/False_positive "False positive") indications of drug use and were the basis of [a legal defense](/wiki/Poppy_seed_defence "Poppy seed defence").[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) On November 30, 1998, the standard cutoff was increased to 2000 nanograms (two micrograms) per milliliter.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Confirmation by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry will distinguish amongst opium and variants including poppy seeds, heroin, and morphine and codeine pharmaceuticals by measuring the morphine:codeine ratio and looking for the presence of noscapine and acetylcodeine, the latter of which is only found in illicitly produced heroin, and heroin metabolites such as 6-monoacetylmorphine.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

### Harvesting and processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Harvesting and processing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Harvesting_opium.jpg/250px-Harvesting_opium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvesting_opium.jpg)

Harvesting opium

When grown for opium production, the skin of the ripening pods of these poppies is scored by a sharp blade at a time carefully chosen so that rain, wind, and dew cannot spoil the exudation of white, milky [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex"), usually in the afternoon. Incisions are made while the pods are still raw, with no more than a slight yellow tint, and must be shallow to avoid penetrating hollow inner chambers or *loculi* while cutting into the lactiferous vessels. In the Indian Subcontinent, Afghanistan, Central Asia and Iran, the special tool used to make the incisions is called a *nushtar* or "nishtar" (from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), meaning a lancet) and carries three or four blades three millimeters apart, which are scored upward along the pod. Incisions are made three or four times at intervals of two to three days, and each time the "poppy tears", which dry to a sticky brown resin, are collected the following morning. One acre harvested in this way can produce three to five kilograms of raw opium.[[128]](#cite_note-opioids.com-128) In the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), pods were typically scored horizontally, and opium was collected three times, or else one or two collections were followed by isolation of opiates from the ripe capsules. Oil poppies, an alternative strain of *P. somniferum*, were also used for production of opiates from their capsules and stems.[[129]](#cite_note-129) A traditional Chinese method of harvesting opium latex involved cutting off the heads and piercing them with a coarse needle then collecting the dried opium 24 to 48 hours later.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%82_%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%B0_%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2_Flower_of_the_Opium.jpg/250px-%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%82_%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%B0_%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2_Flower_of_the_Opium.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E0%A6%86%E0%A6%AB%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%82_%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%B0_%E0%A6%AB%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%B2_Flower_of_the_Opium.jpg)

Poppy flower

Raw opium may be sold to a merchant or broker on the black market, but it usually does not travel far from the field before it is refined into morphine base, because the pungent, jelly-like form of raw opium is bulkier and harder to smuggle. Crude laboratories in the field are capable of refining opium into morphine base by a simple [acid-base extraction](/wiki/Acid-base_extraction "Acid-base extraction"). A sticky, brown paste, morphine base is pressed into bricks and sun-dried, and can either be smoked, prepared into other forms or processed into heroin.[[13]](#cite_note-Frontline-13)

Other methods of preparation (besides smoking), include processing into regular opium [tincture](/wiki/Tincture "Tincture") (*tinctura opii*), [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum"), [paregoric](/wiki/Paregoric "Paregoric") (*tinctura opii camphorata*), [herbal wine](/wiki/Herbal_wine "Herbal wine") (e.g., *vinum opii*), opium powder (*pulvis opii*), opium [sirup](/wiki/Sirup "Sirup") (*sirupus opii*) and opium extract (*extractum opii*).[[130]](#cite_note-Belgische-130) Vinum opii is made by combining sugar, [white wine](/wiki/White_wine "White wine"), [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon"), and [cloves](/wiki/Cloves "Cloves"). Opium syrup is made by combining 97.5 part sugar syrup with 2.5 parts opium extract. Opium extract (*extractum opii*) finally can be made by [macerating](/wiki/Macerating "Macerating") raw opium with water. To make opium extract, 20 parts water are combined with 1 part raw opium which has been boiled for 5 minutes (the latter to ease mixing).[[130]](#cite_note-Belgische-130)

Heroin is widely preferred because of increased potency. One study in postaddicts found heroin to be approximately 2.2 times more potent than [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") by weight with a similar duration; at these relative quantities, they could distinguish the drugs subjectively but had no preference.[[131]](#cite_note-131) Heroin was also found to be twice as potent as morphine in surgical anesthesia.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Morphine is converted into heroin by a simple chemical reaction with [acetic anhydride](/wiki/Acetic_anhydride "Acetic anhydride"), followed by purification.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-UNODC_2005-134) Especially in Mexican production, opium may be converted directly to "[black tar heroin](/wiki/Black_tar_heroin "Black tar heroin")" in a simplified procedure. This form predominates in the U.S. west of the Mississippi. Relative to other preparations of heroin, it has been associated with a dramatically decreased rate of HIV transmission among [intravenous drug users](/wiki/Intravenous_drug_use "Intravenous drug use") (4 percent in Los Angeles vs. 40 percent in New York) due to technical requirements of injection, although it is also associated with greater risk of venous [sclerosis](/wiki/Sclerosis_(medicine) "Sclerosis (medicine)") and [necrotizing fasciitis](/wiki/Necrotizing_fasciitis "Necrotizing fasciitis").[[135]](#cite_note-135)

### Illegal production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Illegal production")]

See also: [Opium production in Afghanistan](/wiki/Opium_production_in_Afghanistan "Opium production in Afghanistan") and [Illegal drug trade](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade "Illegal drug trade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/CIA_Map_of_International_illegal_drug_connections.gif/250px-CIA_Map_of_International_illegal_drug_connections.gif)](/wiki/File:CIA_Map_of_International_illegal_drug_connections.gif)

International drug routes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Opium_poppies_in_Helmand_-a.jpg/250px-Opium_poppies_in_Helmand_-a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opium_poppies_in_Helmand_-a.jpg)

Afghanistan, [Helmand province](/wiki/Helmand_province "Helmand province"). A [Marine](/wiki/United_States_Marines "United States Marines") greets local children working in the field of opium poppies near the base.

[Afghanistan](/wiki/Opium_production_in_Afghanistan "Opium production in Afghanistan") was formerly the primary producer of the drug. Having regularly producing 70 percent of the world's opium, Afghanistan decreased production to 74 tons per year under a ban by the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") in 2000, a move which cut production by 94 percent. A year later, after American and British troops [invaded Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)"), removed the Taliban and installed the interim government, the land under cultivation leapt back to 285 square miles (740 km2), with Afghanistan supplanting Burma to become the world's largest opium producer once more. Opium production increased rapidly in Afghanistan from that point,[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) reaching an all-time high in 2006. According to [DEA](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration") statistics, Afghanistan's production of oven-dried opium increased to 1,278 tons in 2002, more than doubled by 2003, and nearly doubled again during 2004. In late 2004, the U.S. government estimated that 206,000 hectares were under poppy cultivation, 4.5 percent of the country's total cropland, and produced 4,200 metric tons of opium, 76 percent of the world's supply, yielding 60 percent of Afghanistan's gross domestic product.[[138]](#cite_note-138) In 2006, the [UN Office on Drugs and Crime](/wiki/UN_Office_on_Drugs_and_Crime "UN Office on Drugs and Crime") estimated production to have risen 59 percent to 165,000 hectares (407,000 acres) in cultivation, yielding 6,100 tons of opium, 82 percent of the world's supply.[[139]](#cite_note-139) The value of the resulting heroin was estimated at US$3.5 billion, of which Afghan farmers were estimated to have received US$700 million in revenue. For farmers, the crop can be up to ten times more profitable than wheat. The price of opium is around US$138 per kilo. Opium production has led to rising tensions in Afghan villages. Though direct conflict has yet to occur, the opinions of the new class of young rich men involved in the opium trade are at odds with those of the traditional village leaders.[[140]](#cite_note-Goodhand-2000-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Afghanistan_opium_poppy_cultivation_1994-2007b.PNG/250px-Afghanistan_opium_poppy_cultivation_1994-2007b.PNG)](/wiki/File:Afghanistan_opium_poppy_cultivation_1994-2007b.PNG)

Afghanistan opium poppy cultivation, 1994–2016 (hectares)

An increasingly large fraction of opium is processed into morphine base and heroin in drug labs in Afghanistan. Despite an international set of chemical controls designed to restrict availability of [acetic anhydride](/wiki/Acetic_anhydride "Acetic anhydride"), it enters the country, perhaps through its Central Asian neighbors which do not participate. A counternarcotics law passed in December 2005 requires Afghanistan to develop registries or regulations for tracking, storing, and owning acetic anhydride.[[141]](#cite_note-141) In November 2023, a U.N report showed that in the entirety of Afghanistan, poppy cultivation dropped by over 95%, removing it from its place as being the world's largest opium producer.[[120]](#cite_note-aljazeera.com-120)[[121]](#cite_note-news.un.org-121)

Besides Afghanistan, smaller quantities of opium are produced in Pakistan, the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_(Southeast_Asia) "Golden Triangle (Southeast Asia)") region of Southeast Asia (particularly [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")), Colombia, Guatemala, and Mexico.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Opiumball.jpg/250px-Opiumball.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opiumball.jpg)

200 g (7.1 oz) Spanish opium ball

Chinese production mainly trades with and profits from North America. In 2002, they were seeking to expand through eastern United States. In the post 9/11 era, trading between borders became difficult and because new international laws were set into place, the opium trade became more diffused. Power shifted from remote to high-end smugglers and opium traders. Outsourcing became a huge factor for survival for many smugglers and opium farmers.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

In 2023 [Burma](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") overtook [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and became the world's largest producer of opium, producing 1080 metric tones according the [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") Southeast Asia Opium Survey report.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

### Legal production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Legal production")]

Main article: [Opium licensing](/wiki/Opium_licensing "Opium licensing")

Legal opium production is allowed under the [United Nations Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs](/wiki/United_Nations_Single_Convention_on_Narcotic_Drugs "United Nations Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs") and other international drug treaties, subject to strict supervision by the [law enforcement agencies](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency") of individual countries. The leading legal production method is the Robertson-[Gregory process](/w/index.php?title=Gregory_process&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gregory process (page does not exist)"), whereby the entire poppy, excluding roots and leaves, is mashed and stewed in dilute acid solutions. The [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloids "Alkaloids") are then recovered via [acid-base extraction](/wiki/Acid-base_extraction "Acid-base extraction") and purified. The exact date of its discovery is unknown, but it was described by Wurtz in his *Dictionnaire de chimie pure et appliquée* published in 1868.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

[Legal opium production in India](/wiki/Legal_opium_production_in_India "Legal opium production in India") is much more traditional. As of 2008, opium was collected by farmers who were licensed to grow 0.1 hectares (0.25 acres) of opium poppies, who to maintain their licences needed to sell 56 kilograms of unadulterated raw opium paste. The price of opium paste is fixed by the government according to the quality and quantity tendered. The average is around 1500 rupees (US$29) per kilogram.[[145]](#cite_note-145) Some additional money is made by drying the poppy heads and collecting poppy seeds, and a small fraction of opium beyond the quota may be consumed locally or diverted to the black market. The opium paste is dried and processed into government opium and alkaloid factories before it is packed into cases of 60 kilograms for export. Purification of chemical constituents is done in India for domestic production, but typically done abroad by foreign importers.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Legal opium importation from India and Turkey is conducted by [Mallinckrodt](/wiki/Mallinckrodt "Mallinckrodt"), [Noramco](/wiki/Noramco "Noramco"), [Abbott Laboratories](/wiki/Abbott_Laboratories "Abbott Laboratories"), [Purdue Pharma](/wiki/Purdue_Pharma "Purdue Pharma"), and [Cody Laboratories Inc.](/w/index.php?title=Cody_Laboratories_Inc.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cody Laboratories Inc. (page does not exist)") in the United States, and legal opium production is conducted by [GlaxoSmithKline](/wiki/GlaxoSmithKline "GlaxoSmithKline"), [Johnson & Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_%26_Johnson "Johnson & Johnson"), [Johnson Matthey](/wiki/Johnson_Matthey "Johnson Matthey"), and [Mayne](/wiki/Mayne_Pharma "Mayne Pharma") in [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), Australia; [Sanofi Aventis](/wiki/Sanofi_Aventis "Sanofi Aventis") in France; [Shionogi](/wiki/Shionogi "Shionogi") Pharmaceutical in Japan; and [MacFarlan Smith](/wiki/MacFarlan_Smith "MacFarlan Smith") in the United Kingdom.[[147]](#cite_note-Senlis-147) The UN treaty requires that every country submit annual reports to the [International Narcotics Control Board](/wiki/International_Narcotics_Control_Board "International Narcotics Control Board"), stating that year's actual consumption of many classes of controlled drugs as well as opioids and projecting required quantities for the next year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This is to allow trends in consumption to be monitored and production quotas allotted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2005, the European [Senlis Council](/wiki/Senlis_Council "Senlis Council") began developing a programme which hopes to solve the problems caused by the large quantity of [opium produced illegally in Afghanistan](/wiki/Opium_production_in_Afghanistan "Opium production in Afghanistan"), most of which is converted to heroin and smuggled for sale in Europe and the United States.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This proposal is to [license](/wiki/Opium_licensing "Opium licensing") Afghan farmers to produce opium for the world pharmaceutical market, and thereby solve another problem, that of chronic underuse of potent analgesics where required within [developing nations](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"). Part of the proposal is to overcome the "80–20 rule" that requires the U.S. to purchase 80 percent of its legal opium from India and Turkey to include Afghanistan, by establishing a second-tier system of supply control that complements the current INCB regulated supply and demand system by providing poppy-based medicines to countries who cannot meet their demand under the current regulations. Senlis arranged a conference in Kabul that brought drug policy experts from around the world to meet with Afghan government officials to discuss internal security, corruption issues, and legal issues within Afghanistan.[[148]](#cite_note-148)
In June 2007, the council launched a "Poppy for Medicines" project that provides a technical blueprint for the implementation of an integrated control system within Afghan village-based poppy for medicine projects: the idea promotes the economic diversification by redirecting proceeds from the legal cultivation of poppy and production of poppy-based medicines.[[149]](#cite_note-senliscouncil-149) There has been criticism of the Senlis report findings by Macfarlan Smith, who argue that though they produce morphine in Europe, they were never asked to contribute to the report.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

#### Cultivation in the UK

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cultivation in the UK")]

In late 2006, the British government permitted the pharmaceutical company [MacFarlan Smith](/wiki/MacFarlan_Smith "MacFarlan Smith") (a [Johnson Matthey](/wiki/Johnson_Matthey "Johnson Matthey") company) to cultivate opium poppies in England for medicinal reasons, after Macfarlan Smith's primary source, India, decided to increase the price of export opium latex. This move is well received by British farmers, with a major opium poppy field located in [Didcot](/wiki/Didcot "Didcot"), England. The British government has contradicted the Home Office's suggestion that opium cultivation can be legalized in Afghanistan for exports to the United Kingdom, helping lower poverty and internal fighting while helping the [NHS](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") to meet the high demand for [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and heroin. Opium poppy cultivation in the United Kingdom does not need a licence, but a licence is required for those wishing to extract opium for medicinal products.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### Consumption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Consumption")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Akha_man_with_opium_pipe.jpg/250px-Akha_man_with_opium_pipe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Akha_man_with_opium_pipe.jpg)

An [Akha](/wiki/Akha_people "Akha people") man smokes a pipe containing opium mixed with tobacco.

In the industrialized world, the United States is the world's biggest consumer of prescription opioids, with Italy being one of the lowest, because of tighter regulations on prescribing narcotics for pain relief.[[152]](#cite_note-152) Most opium imported into the United States is broken down into its [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") constituents, and whether legal or illegal, most current drug use occurs with processed derivatives such as heroin rather than with unrefined opium.

[Intravenous injection](/wiki/Intravenous_injection "Intravenous injection") of opiates is most used: by comparison with injection, "dragon chasing" (heating of heroin on a piece of foil), and [madak](/wiki/Madak "Madak") and "ack ack" (smoking of cigarettes containing tobacco mixed with heroin powder) are only 40 percent and 20 percent efficient, respectively.[[153]](#cite_note-153) One study of British heroin addicts found a 12-fold excess mortality ratio (1.8 percent of the group dying per year).[[154]](#cite_note-154) Most heroin deaths result not from overdose *per se*, but combination with other depressant drugs such as [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") or [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepine "Benzodiazepine").[[155]](#cite_note-155)

The smoking of opium does not involve the [burning](/wiki/Pyrolysis "Pyrolysis") of the material as might be imagined. Rather, the prepared opium is indirectly heated to temperatures at which the active alkaloids, chiefly morphine, are vaporized. In the past, smokers would use a specially designed [opium pipe](/wiki/Opium_pipe "Opium pipe") which had a removable knob-like pipe-bowl of fired earthenware attached by a metal fitting to a long, cylindrical stem.[[156]](#cite_note-156) A small "pill" of opium about the size of a pea would be placed on the pipe-bowl, which was then heated by holding it over an [opium lamp](/wiki/Opium_lamp "Opium lamp"), a special oil lamp with a distinct funnel-like chimney to channel heat into a small area. The smoker would lie on his or her side in order to guide the pipe-bowl and the tiny pill of opium over the stream of heat rising from the chimney of the oil lamp and inhale the vaporized opium fumes as needed. Several pills of opium were smoked at a single session depending on the smoker's tolerance to the drug. The effects could last up to twelve hours.

In [Eastern culture](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world"), opium is more commonly used in the form of [paregoric](/wiki/Paregoric "Paregoric") to treat [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea"). This is a weaker solution than [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum"), an alcoholic tincture which was prevalently used as a pain medication and sleeping aid. Tincture of opium has been prescribed for, among other things, severe diarrhea.[[157]](#cite_note-157) Taken thirty minutes prior to meals, it significantly slows intestinal motility, giving the intestines greater time to absorb fluid in the stool.

Despite the historically negative view of opium as a cause of addiction, the use of morphine and other derivatives isolated from opium in the treatment of chronic pain has been reestablished. If given in controlled doses, modern opiates can be an effective treatment for [neuropathic pain](/wiki/Neuropathic_pain "Neuropathic pain") and other forms of chronic pain.[[158]](#cite_note-BallantyneJane-158)

## Chemical and physiological properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Chemical and physiological properties")]

See also: [Opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid"), [Opiate](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate"), and [Morphinan](/wiki/Morphinan "Morphinan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Morphin_-_Morphine.svg/120px-Morphin_-_Morphine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Morphin_-_Morphine.svg)

[Morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") is the primary biologically active chemical constituent of opium.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Codein_-_Codeine.svg/120px-Codein_-_Codeine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Codein_-_Codeine.svg)

[Codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") is another biologically active chemical constituent of opium.

Opium contains two main groups of [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid"). [Phenanthrenes](/wiki/Phenanthrene "Phenanthrene") such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), and [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") are the main psychoactive constituents.[[159]](#cite_note-159) [Isoquinolines](/wiki/Isoquinolines "Isoquinolines") such as [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine") have no significant [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") effects. Morphine is the most prevalent and important alkaloid in opium, consisting of 10–16 percent of the total, and is responsible for most of its harmful effects such as [lung edema](/wiki/Lung_edema "Lung edema"), respiratory difficulties, coma, or cardiac or respiratory collapse. Morphine binds to and activates [mu opioid receptors](/wiki/Mu_opioid_receptor "Mu opioid receptor") in the brain, spinal cord, stomach and intestine. Regular use can lead to [drug tolerance](/wiki/Drug_tolerance "Drug tolerance") or [physical dependence](/wiki/Physical_dependence "Physical dependence"). Chronic opium addicts in 1906 China[[53]](#cite_note-McCoy_opium-53) consumed an average of eight grams of opium daily; opium addicts in modern Iran[[160]](#cite_note-160) are thought to consume about the same.

Both [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia") and [drug addiction](/wiki/Drug_addiction "Drug addiction") are functions of the mu opioid receptor, the class of [opioid receptor](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") first identified as responsive to morphine. Tolerance is associated with the superactivation of the receptor, which may be affected by the degree of [endocytosis](/wiki/Endocytosis "Endocytosis") caused by the [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") administered, and leads to a superactivation of [cyclic AMP](/wiki/Cyclic_AMP "Cyclic AMP") signaling.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Long-term use of morphine in [palliative care](/wiki/Palliative_care "Palliative care") and the management of [chronic pain](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain") always entails a risk that the patient develops tolerance or physical dependence. There are many kinds of [rehabilitation treatment](/wiki/Drug_rehabilitation "Drug rehabilitation"), including pharmacologically based treatments with [naltrexone](/wiki/Naltrexone "Naltrexone"), [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone"), or [ibogaine](/wiki/Ibogaine "Ibogaine").[[162]](#cite_note-162)

In 2021, the [International Agency for Research on Cancer](/wiki/International_Agency_for_Research_on_Cancer "International Agency for Research on Cancer") concluded that opium is a Group 1 (sufficient evidence) human carcinogen, causing cancers of the larynx, lung, and urinary bladder.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

## Slang terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Slang terms")]

Some slang terms for opium include: "Big O", "Shanghai Sally", "dope", "hop", "midnight oil", "O.P.", and "tar". "Dope" and "tar" can also refer to heroin. The traditional opium pipe is known as a "dream stick."[[164]](#cite_note-164) The term *dope* entered the English language in the early nineteenth century, originally referring to viscous liquids, particularly sauces or gravy.[[165]](#cite_note-Wordorigins-165)  It has been used to refer to opiates since at least 1888, and this usage arose because opium, when prepared for smoking, is viscous.[[165]](#cite_note-Wordorigins-165)
